title: Recursion & Backtracking
description: Build recursive intuition and master backtracking templates for permutations, subsets, and constraint-satisfaction puzzles that unlock Codeforces 1400–1600 problems.

sections.content: 🎯 The Competitive Programming Mindset

Recursion is not just a programming technique — it is a **problem decomposition philosophy**. When you cannot see how to iterate to a solution, ask: *can I reduce this problem to a smaller version of itself?* Backtracking extends this idea: explore a candidate solution, and if it violates a constraint, **undo the last choice** and try the next. The key skill is identifying what the *state* is, what the *choices* are at each step, and when to *prune* — cutting branches that can never lead to a valid answer.

sections.label: Backtracking Template — The Universal Shape
sections.content: Every backtracking solution has the same skeleton: (1) **base case** — if state is complete/valid, record/return; (2) **loop over choices**; (3) **make choice** (modify state); (4) **recurse**; (5) **undo choice** (restore state). Steps 3 and 5 are the `choose / unchoose` pair that defines backtracking.
🔁 Recursion Fundamentals — The Call Stack Model

Every recursive call pushes a **stack frame** holding local variables and the return address. Deep recursion risks a **stack overflow**. In contests, recursion depth up to ~10⁵ is usually fine in C++ with the default stack, but Codeforces problems with n ≤ 10⁶ may require iterative solutions or explicit stack simulation.

sections.title: Anatomy of a Recursive Function — Fibonacci with Trace

sections.label: 🚨 Contest Killer: Exponential Recursion Without Memoization
sections.content: Naive recursive Fibonacci has time complexity O(2ⁿ). For n=40 that is ~10¹² calls — an instant TLE. Whenever you write a recursive function that calls itself with overlapping subproblems, **always memoize** (or convert to DP). The test: draw the recursion tree — if you see the same arguments computed multiple times, memoization is mandatory.
📐 Pattern 1 — Generate All Subsets (Power Set)
For an array of n elements, there are 2ⁿ subsets. At each index we make a binary choice: **include** or **exclude** the current element. The recursion tree has depth n and 2ⁿ leaves.

sections.title: Generate All Subsets — Include/Exclude Recursion

sections.content: 📐 Pattern 2 — Generate All Permutations

For n elements, there are n! permutations. Track which elements have been **used** with a boolean visited array (or by swapping in-place). The recursion tree has depth n with branching factor decreasing from n down to 1.

sections.title: Generate All Permutations — Visited Array
In-Place Swap Permutations (Space-Efficient)

sections.content: 📐 Pattern 3 — Combination Sum (With Pruning)

Find all combinations of numbers that sum to a target. Key optimization: sort the array first, then **prune** when the remaining candidates can never reach the target (current sum + next candidate already exceeds target). This dramatically reduces the search space.

sections.title: Combination Sum — Reuse Allowed, With Pruning

sections.label: Pruning is the Difference Between TLE and AC
sections.content: In backtracking, **pruning** means detecting early that the current partial solution cannot lead to any valid complete solution, and returning immediately. Common pruning strategies: (1) bound check — partial sum already exceeds target; (2) feasibility — not enough remaining elements to fill required positions; (3) symmetry breaking — skip duplicate candidates in sorted arrays with `if (i > idx && cands[i] == cands[i-1]) continue`.
📐 Pattern 4 — N-Queens Problem

Place n queens on an n×n chessboard such that no two queens threaten each other. This is the canonical **constraint-satisfaction** backtracking problem. Place one queen per row; for each column choice in that row, check row, column, and diagonal conflicts before recursing.

sections.title: N-Queens — Column and Diagonal Conflict Tracking

sections.content: 📐 Pattern 5 — Grid/Graph Path Backtracking

Many Codeforces problems ask for paths, words, or traversals on grids where you must visit cells without repeating. The standard approach is DFS with a **visited** array that is marked before recursion and unmarked after (backtracking).

sections.title: Count All Paths from Top-Left to Bottom-Right

sections.content: 📊 Complexity Reference Table
🧩 Deduplication in Backtracking

When the input has **duplicate elements**, naive permutation/subset generation produces duplicate results. Fix: sort first, then skip a candidate if it is the same as the previous candidate **at the same recursion level** (same depth in the tree, same loop index start).

sections.title: Subsets with Duplicate Elements — Skip Duplicates at Same Level

sections.label: i > idx vs i > start — The Deduplication Condition
sections.content: The skip condition must be `i > idx` (or `i > start`, whatever the starting index parameter is called), **not** `i > 0`. Using `i > 0` would incorrectly skip elements that are the same as a previous element from a **parent** level, removing valid unique subsets. Only skip when the duplicate appears at the **same level** of the recursion tree.
🏋️ Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Trace a Recursive Subset Build
sections.explanation: Trace the include/exclude binary tree for [1,2]: go(0): include 1 → go(1): include 2 → go(2): cur=[1,2] → print '12'. Backtrack: exclude 2 → go(2): cur=[1] → print '1'. Backtrack: exclude 1 → go(1): include 2 → go(2): cur=[2] → print '2'. Backtrack: exclude 2 → go(2): cur=[] → print 'empty'. All 2²=4 subsets printed in include-first order.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Trace Permutation Swap Output
Exercise 3: Generate All Valid Parentheses
sections.approach: Track `open` (number of '(' placed so far) and `close` (number of ')' placed). At each step: if open < n, we can place '('; if close < open, we can place ')'. When the string length reaches 2n, it's complete. This is backtracking with feasibility pruning — we never build an invalid string.
sections.explanation: The constraint `close < open` guarantees we never place ')' before a matching '(', so every string generated is valid. The constraint `open < n` prevents placing more '(' than allowed. Every path from root to leaf (length 2n) is exactly one valid combination. The number of valid combinations is the Catalan number C(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1). For n=3: C(3)=5 strings. Time: O(C(n)*n) ≈ O(4ⁿ/n^1.5 * n). No invalid strings are ever built, so pruning is perfect.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Rat in a Maze — Count All Paths
sections.approach: DFS backtracking from (0,0). At each cell, try going RIGHT (r, c+1) and DOWN (r+1, c). Only move to cells with value 1. Mark visited to avoid revisiting (though with only down/right moves, revisiting is impossible — but for the 4-direction version, always use visited). Increment count when (n-1, n-1) is reached.
sections.explanation: For down/right-only movement there are no cycles, so visited isn't strictly needed — but it's included as best practice for the 4-direction extension. Time complexity: O(2^(R+C)) in the worst case (fully open grid), since at each step we choose down or right. For a 3×3 grid there are C(4,2)=6 possible paths if fully open (Pascal's triangle). With the given blocked cells, only 1 path survives.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Subsets Summing to Target (Count)
sections.approach: Standard include/exclude backtracking. At each index, choose to include or exclude the element. Maintain a running sum. When index reaches n, check if sum == target. Prune: if running sum exceeds target (all values positive), stop. Sort array for better pruning.
sections.explanation: Each element is either included or excluded → 2ⁿ leaves in the recursion tree. The pruning `sum > target` (valid because all elements are positive) can cut significant branches. Sorting in ascending order means once sum > target after adding a small element, adding larger elements would only make it worse — allowing the pruning to trigger earlier. Time: O(2ⁿ) worst case, but typically much better with pruning. For n≤20 this runs well within contest time limits.

sections.content: 📋 Quick Reference Summary

rows: Skip same value at same level
